meta.title: PDAI Calculator — Pemphigus Disease Area Index | Skin Lab Pro
meta.description: Free online PDAI calculator for dermatologists. Assess pemphigus severity with the standardized Pemphigus Disease Area Index score.

actions.resetConfirm: Are you sure? All data will be cleared.

skin.title: Skin
skin.scaleTitle: Erosion/Blister or new erythema grading scale:
skin.scale1: 1 — 1-3 lesions, up to 1-2 cm in any diameter, no more than 6 cm
skin.scale2: 2 — 2-3 lesions, at least 2 lesions >2 cm in diameter, no more than 6 cm
skin.scale3: 3 — more than 3 lesions, no more than 6 cm in diameter
skin.scale5: 5 — more than 3 lesions, and/or at least 1 lesion >6 cm
skin.scale10: 10 — more than 3 lesions, and/or at least 1 lesion >16 cm in diameter or entire area
skin.lesionDesc: When the number of lesions is ≤3 elements, with 1 element ≥2 cm in diameter, but no elements ≥6 cm:
skin.pigmentationTitle: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or erythema of resolved lesion:

scalp.title: Scalp
scalp.scaleTitle: Erosion/Blister or new erythema grading scale:

mucosa.title: Mucous Membranes
mucosa.scale5: 5 — more than 3 lesions or 2 lesions >2 cm
mucosa.noteText: For mucous membranes, the number of lesions is included in the main score. Additional lesion counting is not required.

info.title: About PDAI
info.description: PDAI (Pemphigus Disease Area Index) is a standardized scale for assessing pemphigus severity, developed by an international expert committee.
info.attribution: The PDAI scoring system was developed by the International Pemphigus Definitions Committee, led by Dedee F. Murrell and Victoria P. Werth.
info.citation: Murrell DF, Dick S, Ahmed AR, et al. Consensus statement on definitions of disease, end points, and therapeutic response for pemphigus. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2008;58(6):1043-1046.
info.digitalTool: This web application is a digital implementation of the published scoring system and is not affiliated with the original methodology authors.
info.disclaimer: This calculator is intended for use by medical professionals and does not replace clinical judgment.

footer.disclaimer: Digital implementation of the PDAI scale (Murrell et al., 2008)

mild.treatment:
1. Systemic therapy: Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day with gradual tapering.
2. Steroid-sparing therapy: Azathioprine 1–3 mg/kg/day or Mycophenolate mofetil 2–3 g/day.
3. Topical therapy: Topical corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05%) on erosions and blisters; antiseptic solutions (chlorhexidine 0.05%) for erosion care.
mild.management:
• Outpatient dermatologist follow-up every 2–4 weeks.
• Complete blood count and biochemistry monitoring every 2–4 weeks.
• Anti-desmoglein antibody titer monitoring (every 3 months).
• PDAI assessment at each visit.
mild.nutrition:
• Soft diet if mucosal involvement (pureed food, avoid spicy, acidic, hot foods).
• Adequate protein intake (1.2–1.5 g/kg/day) for tissue repair.
• Calcium (1000–1200 mg/day) and Vitamin D (800–1000 IU/day) for osteoporosis prevention during corticosteroid therapy.

moderate.treatment:
1. Systemic therapy: Prednisolone 1–1.5 mg/kg/day.
2. Immunosuppression: Azathioprine 2–3 mg/kg/day or Mycophenolate mofetil 2–3 g/day; consider Rituximab if insufficient response.
3. Topical therapy: High-potency topical corticosteroids; antiseptic erosion care; for oral involvement — dexamethasone rinse solution.
moderate.management:
• Consider hospitalization if rapidly progressive.
• Dermatologist evaluation every 1–2 weeks.
• Weekly complete blood count and biochemistry monitoring.
• Anti-desmoglein antibody titer monitoring (every 1–3 months).
• PDAI assessment at each visit, photodocumentation of lesions.
moderate.nutrition:
• High-protein diet (1.5–2 g/kg/day protein).
• Liquid or semi-liquid food consistency if significant mucosal involvement.
• Increased calcium and Vitamin D supplementation.
• Glycemic control (limit simple carbohydrates during high-dose corticosteroid therapy).

severe.treatment:
1. Systemic therapy: Pulse methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg/day IV for 3 days) followed by Prednisolone 1.5–2 mg/kg/day.
2. Biologic therapy: Rituximab (1000 mg IV, 2 infusions 2 weeks apart) — consider as first-line therapy.
3. Immunosuppression: Cyclophosphamide or Azathioprine + Mycophenolate mofetil.
4. IVIG: Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg per cycle for refractory disease.
5. Topical therapy: Specialized wound dressings; antiseptic care; pain management.
severe.management:
• Mandatory hospitalization.
• Multidisciplinary approach (dermatologist, internist, nutritionist; ophthalmologist if ocular involvement).
• Daily monitoring: fluid-electrolyte balance, glycemia, blood pressure, temperature.
• Blood work 2–3 times per week.
• Infection prevention (wound cultures, antibiotics if needed).
• Thromboembolic complication prophylaxis.
severe.nutrition:
• High-calorie, high-protein diet (2–2.5 g/kg/day protein).
• If oral feeding is impossible — nasogastric tube or parenteral nutrition.
• Enhanced nutritional support: B vitamins, zinc, Vitamin C.
• Strict glycemic control.
• Adequate hydration (fluid balance monitoring).

general.title: General Recommendations
general.content:
• Avoid skin trauma (Nikolsky sign phenomenon).
• Wear soft, loose clothing made of natural fabrics.
• Avoid UV exposure (SPF 50+ sunscreen).
• Oral hygiene: soft toothbrush, non-abrasive toothpaste.
• Osteoporosis prevention during long-term corticosteroid therapy.
• Vaccination: pneumococcal, influenza (before immunosuppression or during remission).
• Psychological support for the patient.
• Patient education: recognizing flares, skin care protocols.
• Regular photodocumentation of lesions for monitoring.
